fast.summary: PLUM adapts pre-trained LLMs for large-scale recommendation by combining semantic item tokenization, domain-specific continued pre-training, and generative retrieval fine-tuning to directly generate recommended item IDs from user context. The framework was deployed on YouTube at billion-user scale, outperforming heavily-optimized production models with large embedding tables.
fast.key_idea: Instead of learning item embeddings from scratch, LLMs can be fine-tuned to autoregressively generate Semantic IDs of relevant items, leveraging world knowledge and sequence modeling to perform retrieval without explicit embedding lookup.
fast.why_it_matters: This shows LLM-based generative retrieval can beat traditional embedding-table recommenders at industrial scale, offering a path to replace or augment classic two-tower/ANN retrieval pipelines with a single generative model that benefits from LLM pretraining.
possible_use_cases: Replacing or augmenting two-tower retrieval models in large-scale recommendation systems with a generative LLM backbone
Cold-start recommendation for new items by leveraging LLM world knowledge rather than relying on learned embeddings
Unified retrieval and ranking pipeline where a single fine-tuned LLM handles candidate generation using semantic item representations

deep.method_breakdown: PLUM adapts pre-trained LLMs for recommendation via three stages: (1) item tokenization using Semantic IDs (hierarchical discrete codes derived from item embeddings via RQ-VAE), (2) continued pre-training (CPT) on domain-specific data to bridge the distribution gap between general text and recommendation signals, and (3) task-specific fine-tuning for generative retrieval where the model autoregressively generates Semantic ID sequences of recommended items given user context. At inference, beam search over the Semantic ID vocabulary retrieves candidate items directly without embedding table lookups.
deep.key_innovation: The primary innovation is demonstrating that a generative LLM-based retrieval system can outperform heavily-optimized production embedding-table models at YouTube scale (billions of users). Prior work on generative retrieval (TIGER, GENRE) validated the concept on academic benchmarks; PLUM is the first to show scaling laws for this paradigm in a real industrial deployment, with enhancements to Semantic IDs (e.g., improved codebook utilization, collision handling) and CPT strategies tailored to close the domain gap for video recommendation.
deep.technical_insight: The most critical design choice is Semantic ID construction via RQ-VAE over item content embeddings rather than purely collaborative-filtering-based IDs. This grounds item tokens in semantic meaning, allowing the LLM's pre-trained world knowledge to transfer — items with similar content share prefix codes, so the model can generalize to long-tail and cold-start items. Without semantic grounding, the LLM's language priors are wasted on arbitrary ID tokens and the autoregressive factorization loses its structural advantage.
deep.limitations: Generative retrieval via beam search over Semantic IDs is inherently constrained by codebook coverage — items not well-represented by learned codes suffer retrieval degradation. The approach has high training compute cost (LLM-scale CPT + fine-tuning) and beam search latency at inference, making real-time serving engineering complex. Semantic IDs also introduce a quantization bottleneck: information lost during RQ-VAE compression cannot be recovered downstream. The paper focuses on YouTube video recommendation, so generalization to sparse-interaction domains (e-commerce cold start, new platforms) remains unvalidated.
interview_questions: Why use Semantic IDs rather than directly generating item text titles or raw item IDs, and what are the trade-offs?
How does continued pre-training (CPT) on domain-specific data help, and what data mixture did you find most effective?
During beam search for generative retrieval, how do you ensure retrieved Semantic IDs map to valid/existing items, and how do you handle codebook collisions?
How did PLUM's scaling behavior compare to traditional embedding-table models — did retrieval quality scale with model size following a power law?
What are the latency and throughput constraints at inference for a system serving billions of YouTube users, and how were they addressed?
interview_answers: Raw item IDs are arbitrary tokens with no semantic structure, so the LLM cannot leverage pre-trained knowledge — every item must be learned from scratch via collaborative signals, which fails for long-tail items. Text titles are variable-length and cause vocabulary explosion at catalog scale. Semantic IDs via RQ-VAE compress each item into a short fixed-length sequence of discrete codes drawn from a small shared codebook, enabling the LLM to generalize across semantically similar items while keeping the autoregressive output space tractable for beam search.

CPT closes the distribution gap between general web text and the recommendation domain (user watch histories, video metadata, interaction sequences). The most effective mixture balances domain-specific sequential interaction data with item content data; too much generic text dilutes recommendation signal, while too little leaves the model unable to leverage LLM priors. CPT also teaches the model the Semantic ID vocabulary structure before task-specific fine-tuning, which stabilizes training.

Beam search naturally stays within the valid Semantic ID prefix tree constructed from the item catalog, so only existing code sequences can be generated — invalid paths are pruned during search. Collisions (multiple items sharing the same Semantic ID) are addressed through improved codebook utilization techniques such as better RQ-VAE training objectives and post-hoc collision resolution strategies that assign disambiguating suffix codes, ensuring a near-injective mapping from items to ID sequences.

PLUM exhibits clear power-law scaling: retrieval quality (measured by recall@k against production labels) improves consistently with both model parameter count and training compute, similar to LLM scaling laws for language tasks. Crucially, the scaling slope for PLUM exceeded that of the embedding-table baseline at comparable compute budgets, indicating the generative paradigm is a better use of additional parameters for retrieval in this domain.

Autoregressive beam search over long Semantic ID sequences introduces significant per-query latency. The team addressed this through constrained decoding with a trie-based prefix index to prune invalid beams early, model distillation to reduce serving model size, and batched GPU inference with careful KV-cache management. The system was designed so that generative retrieval runs as a candidate generator alongside (not replacing) existing retrieval stacks, allowing latency budgets to be managed by controlling beam width and number of retrieval candidates.
